title.es: Análisis de punto de equilibrio: justificación de función de detección de fraude

question.en: A new fraud-detection feature costs $3M per year in fixed operating expenses to maintain. Each fraud case it successfully prevents averts an average of $40 in direct loss. How many fraud cases per year must the feature prevent to break even? Walk through the calculation and name the assumptions your answer depends on.
question.es: Una nueva función de detección de fraude cuesta $3M por año en gastos operativos fijos para mantener. Cada caso de fraude que previene exitosamente evita una pérdida directa promedio de $40. ¿Cuántos casos de fraude por año debe prevenir la función para alcanzar el punto de equilibrio? Explica el cálculo y nombra las suposiciones de las que depende tu respuesta.

must_include: the explicit breakeven formula applied: breakeven cases = fixed cost / value per prevented case = $3M / $40 = 75,000 cases per year
acknowledgment that $40 is likely an average that masks a distribution — high-value fraud events (account takeover, card-present fraud) may save far more per case while low-value disputes save less

distinction between fraud cases prevented and fraud cases detected — a false-positive rate means some 'prevented' cases are legitimate transactions, which carries its own cost (customer friction, declined spend, potential churn)

the assumption about marginal vs fully-loaded cost: if the $3M includes only incremental cost, the breakeven holds; if shared infrastructure is allocated into the $3M, the true incremental breakeven is lower
at least one named fragility: what would cause the breakeven to move by a factor of 2 or more (e.g., if average averted loss is $20 instead of $40, breakeven doubles to 150K cases)
must_avoid: stopping at the arithmetic answer (75,000) without discussing the assumptions it depends on
confusing total fraud cases in the portfolio with incremental cases the feature would prevent — the feature does not prevent all fraud, only the subset its model catches
ignoring the cost of false positives as a business expense that should factor into the net benefit calculation
